title: The Snakes Have Opinions
tagline: Medusa's snakes have always had opinions. Today the team meets one of them.
hook_line: Perseus brought a letter back from Medusa — the reply to his EP030 letter. The reply has a postscript written in different handwriting. Much smaller. Very direct. It says: 'Perseus — this is SEVEN. I am the one on the left side. Medusa says we cannot visit but I want you to know I have been watching you carry things carefully and I think that is correct. — SEVEN.'

scenes.title: The Letter with the Postscript
scenes.narration: Perseus got Medusa's reply two days after sending his letter. He read it alone first — that was how they did it. She had written about the Titan's reaction to the sculpture (she was glad), about the second sculpture she had placed at the valley overlook (facing east, toward the sea, where the river turned), and about the spring light that had been particularly good lately. Then there was a postscript. The handwriting changed. It was smaller and more vertical. It said: 'Perseus — this is SEVEN. I am the one on the left side. Medusa says we cannot visit but I want you to know I have been watching you carry things carefully and I think that is correct. — SEVEN.' Perseus read the postscript three times.
scenes.visual_prompt: Perseus reading Medusa's letter, holding it carefully — the main letter visible in Medusa's handwriting: elegant, deliberate strokes — and then at the bottom, the postscript in different handwriting: smaller, vertical, very direct — 'this is SEVEN. I am the one on the left side.' — Perseus reading the postscript three times, visible in his expression — the specific quality of receiving a letter that has a second, unexpected correspondent — morning light, solo scene, #FFD700 and warm letter-paper tones, the handwriting change visible as a key visual detail, a small snake head visible in the margin next to the postscript as if SEVEN wrote it directly

scenes.title: Perseus Shows Athena
scenes.narration: Perseus brought the letter to Athena. He showed her the postscript without explaining it. Athena read it. She looked up. 'One of the snakes wrote to you.' 'Yes.' 'SEVEN.' 'Yes.' 'Do you know which one SEVEN is?' Perseus thought about it. 'Left side,' he said. 'Medusa said the left side ones are more curious than the right side ones. The right side ones are more skeptical.' Athena looked at the postscript again. '

What did you carry?' 'The sculpture case.' Athena nodded slowly. 'SEVEN has been watching since the sculpture.' 'Apparently,' Perseus said. 'Since before the sculpture,' Athena said. 'You've been visiting the east for a long time.' Perseus said nothing. Archie slow-blinked from the desk.
scenes.visual_prompt: Perseus and Athena with the letter — Athena reading the postscript without preamble — 'one of the snakes wrote to you. SEVEN.' — Perseus's information about left-side vs right-side snakes (left: curious, right: skeptical) — 'watching you carry things carefully — what did you carry?' — 'the sculpture case' — Athena's correction: 'since before the sculpture. You've been visiting the east for a long time.' — Perseus's silence — Archie on the desk, slow blink — the specific quality of Athena connecting dots she's been collecting for multiple episodes — Athena writing something in her scroll — #FFD700 and Athena's study, the letter visible, Archie's slow blink the punctuation of the scene

scenes.title: Zeus Hears About SEVEN
scenes.narration: The letter made it to the main hall because Athena told Poseidon who mentioned it during the morning briefing. Zeus asked to see it. He read the main letter first, nodding — he liked Medusa's letters, they were specific. Then he read the postscript. His crown listed left. It stayed left for the full duration of reading the postscript, which was short. 'One of the snakes,' he said. 'SEVEN,' Athena said. 'Left side.' 'The curious ones.' 'Yes.' Zeus read it one more time. He looked at Perseus. His crown was still left. 'Write back to SEVEN,' he said. 'Through Medusa. Tell SEVEN that we have also noticed how carefully things are carried here.' Perseus said: 'Should I tell Medusa it's from you?' Zeus's crown listed further left. 'No,' he said. 'Tell SEVEN it's from SEVEN's left side neighbors.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Zeus reading the letter in the main hall — the main letter, nodding (he likes Medusa's letters) — then the postscript — crown listing left, staying left through the full read — 'write back to SEVEN. Through Medusa. Tell SEVEN that we have also noticed how carefully things are carried here.' — Perseus's 'should I say it's from you?' — Zeus's crown listing further left as he thinks — 'no. Tell SEVEN it's from SEVEN's left side neighbors.' — the warmth of Zeus's response: not authoritative, just genuine — Poseidon writing in his log (sardine on the note) — Hercules looking at the letter, wanting to write something to SEVEN too but not saying so — #FFD700 and main hall warmth, Zeus's crown listing detail clear

scenes.title: Hercules Also Wants to Write
scenes.narration: Hercules found Perseus later. He said: 'I also want to write something to SEVEN.' Perseus looked at him. 'I can include it with the reply.' 'Is that allowed?' 'I don't know,' Perseus said. 'SEVEN wrote to me without asking if it was allowed.' Hercules thought about this. 'SEVEN has a point,' he said. He wrote his message. It was three sentences. He read it back: 'Dear SEVEN. I am Hercules. I am also on the left side of things when I can choose.' He looked at it. 'That is what I wanted to say.' Perseus read it. 'That's good,' he said. 'Yes,' Hercules agreed. 'I thought about it carefully.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Hercules finding Perseus to ask about writing to SEVEN — 'is that allowed?' / 'SEVEN wrote to me without asking if it was allowed.' — Hercules's conclusion: 'SEVEN has a point' — Hercules's three-sentence letter: 'Dear SEVEN. I am Hercules. I am also on the left side of things when I can choose.' — reading it back, satisfied — Perseus: 'that's good' / Hercules: 'I thought about it carefully.' — the sincerity of Hercules writing three sentences and being confident they are correct — Artemis nearby, watching, not saying anything — but she has also taken out her notebook — #FFD700 and the warm domestic quality of writing correspondence, Hercules's serious expression over his three sentences

scenes.title: Artemis and Poseidon
scenes.narration: Artemis wrote four words. She gave them to Perseus without context. They said: 'SEVEN: accurate count. — A.' Perseus showed it to Poseidon, who had also written something. Poseidon's message was longer — three paragraphs about the dolphins, the raft, the way the Titan had held the sculpture. He had included a small pressed sardine because he always did and SEVEN was, in his view, part of the correspondence now. Perseus looked at the sardine. 'It will travel,' he said. 'Sardines travel well,' Poseidon said with confidence. 'I have sent many things by dolphin.' Perseus packaged all five messages — Zeus's through SEVEN's left-side neighbors, Hercules's three sentences, Artemis's four words, Poseidon's three paragraphs and sardine, and his own. He addressed the outer envelope to Medusa and the inner note to SEVEN.
scenes.visual_prompt: Artemis's four words: 'SEVEN: accurate count. — A.' given to Perseus without context — Poseidon's three-paragraph letter plus pressed sardine — Perseus looking at the sardine — 'it will travel' / 'sardines travel well. I have sent many things by dolphin.' — the assembly of all five messages: Zeus's (from left-side neighbors), Hercules's three sentences, Artemis's four words, Poseidon's letter and sardine, and Perseus's own — the outer envelope to Medusa, the inner note to SEVEN — the specific charm of an impromptu collaborative letter to a snake — Archie watching the assembly from the counter, having not been asked to contribute but appearing to be considering it — #FFD700 and warm letter-assembly scene, all the different handwriting styles visible in the stack

scenes.title: Medusa's Reply (SEVEN's Postscript)
scenes.narration: Medusa's reply came back four days later. The main letter was about the sardine, which she confirmed had arrived and which SEVEN had strong opinions about (positive). Then: seven postscripts. One from each of the left-side snakes. Perseus read them aloud to whoever was in the main hall. SEVEN: 'I SHOWED THE SARDINE TO THREE AND FOUR. THEY WERE IMPRESSED. — SEVEN.' THREE: 'The sardine was good. I am THREE. — THREE.' FOUR: 'We have also been watching. The careful carrying is consistent. — FOUR (THREE agrees).' FIVE: 'I am on the curious side but I am actually quite skeptical. — FIVE.' TWO: 'Hello. — TWO.' ONE: 'I know everything that happens on the left side. I have been monitoring. — ONE.' And finally, from the snake with no number: 'I don't have a name yet. I am thinking about it. I'll write again.' Zeus's crown was straight for 3 seconds.
scenes.visual_prompt: Medusa's letter with seven postscripts — Perseus reading them aloud in the main hall, the team assembled — the seven messages rendered in seven different tiny handwriting styles: SEVEN's confident caps, THREE's short confirmation, FOUR's group note, FIVE's self-description ('quite skeptical'), TWO's simple 'Hello.', ONE's monitoring announcement, and the unnamed snake still deciding — Zeus's crown straight for 3 seconds at the unnamed snake's message — the team's collective delight at this: the left-side of Medusa's snakes has now formally joined the correspondence — Archie looking at the letters closely — Poseidon marking in his log that the sardine was received well — #FFD700 and main hall warmth, seven postscripts visible as distinct notes at the bottom of Medusa's letter

scenes.title: Archie's Contribution
scenes.narration: The next letter east included a small addition at the bottom — Athena's handwriting, but clearly written by someone else's instruction. It said: 'To the snake who is still deciding on a name: there is an owl here who took a long time deciding on how to act, and then acted very specifically. His name is Archie. He wants you to know that deciding carefully is not the same as not knowing.' Archie was sitting on the desk when Athena sealed the letter. He did not slow-blink. He looked at the letter for a moment. Then he looked at Athena. Then he went back to looking at whatever Archie looks at. Athena added his usual mark — a small feather shape she'd started drawing when he contributed something — at the bottom of his note.
scenes.visual_prompt: The next letter east — the addition at the bottom in Athena's handwriting but ARCHIE's voice: 'to the snake who is still deciding on a name: there is an owl here who took a long time deciding on how to act. His name is Archie. He wants you to know that deciding carefully is not the same as not knowing.' — Archie on the desk as Athena seals the letter — not slow-blinking, just watching the letter — looking at Athena — going back to watching — Athena adding the small feather mark she draws when he contributes — Archie's contribution to the correspondence as the emotional close of the episode — #3D2B00 and warm lamp amber, the letter sealed, Archie and Athena in the lamplight, the feather mark the final detail

viral_hook: Medusa wrote a letter. At the bottom was a postscript in different handwriting: 'Perseus — this is SEVEN. I am the one on the left side. I have been watching you carry things carefully and I think that is correct.' Then the whole team wrote back to SEVEN. Then SEVEN replied with six other left-side snakes' postscripts. The seventh snake doesn't have a name yet. Zeus's crown went straight.
highlight_note: The seven postscripts read aloud: SEVEN, THREE, FOUR, FIVE (skeptical), TWO (just 'Hello.'), ONE (monitoring), and the unnamed one who'll write again when they have a name. Zeus crown straight 3 seconds at the unnamed snake.
short_title: SEVEN Wrote to Perseus and Then the Whole Left Side Got Involved
rewatch_bait: Scene 3 — Zeus's message to SEVEN: 'from SEVEN's left-side neighbors.' He never identifies himself. Then his crown lists further left when he decides. Left-side solidarity from Zeus.
youtube_title: One of Medusa's snakes wrote Perseus a letter. Then all seven left-side snakes wrote back. | Little Olympus S2 EP11
lesson: Curiosity is a good reason to reach out, even if you don't know if it's allowed. SEVEN didn't ask permission. SEVEN just had something to say and said it. Sometimes the best correspondence starts that way.
arc_notes: Medusa character expansion via her snakes — they have individual personalities, numbers, monitoring activities. Left vs right side: curious vs skeptical. The unnamed snake is a S2 ongoing element — will name itself eventually. Archie's contribution: 'deciding carefully is not the same as not knowing' — his voice through Athena's handwriting, the feather mark established as his contribution notation. Zeus crown: lists left for SEVEN's letter, further left for 'left-side neighbors' decision, straight 3 seconds for the unnamed snake. Hercules's three-sentence letter is his best joke this season. Medusa's character: she allowed the snakes to add their postscripts, which tells you everything about how she manages her household.
